form_name: EMO Day Center Kiosk - Phase 1 Discovery
form_description: Discovery questions for the EMO Oregon Day Center kiosk project. Captures operational context, data requirements, hardware decisions, and policy choices needed to finalize the Statement of Work.

sections.name: day_center_today
sections.title: The Day Center Today
sections.description: Understand their world before proposing anything. Daily operations, current sign-in process, services offered.

sections.name: data_and_reporting
sections.title: Data and Reporting
sections.description: What do they need to get OUT of the system? Funding sources, HMIS, RSR, and other reporting obligations.

sections.name: client_data_fields
sections.title: Client Data Fields
sections.description: What exactly goes into the system? Registration fields, HIV status handling, check-in fields.

sections.name: physical_space
sections.title: Physical Space and Workflow
sections.description: Determines hardware and deployment topology. Layout, power, internet, security.

sections.name: hardware_decision
sections.title: Hardware Decision
sections.description: Windows PC with touchscreen. Tier selection, single vs dual screen, new vs refurbished.

sections.name: software_path
sections.title: Software Path
sections.description: C# native vs WSL2/Python. Implementation detail but affects maintainability and portability.

sections.name: access_control
sections.title: Access Control and Roles
sections.description: Who can do what in the system? Staff accounts, permission levels, client check-in flow.

sections.name: policy_decisions
sections.title: Policy Decisions
sections.description: Data retention, backup rotation, open source vs proprietary, encryption key custody, incident procedures.

sections.name: timeline_logistics
sections.title: Timeline and Logistics
sections.description: When operational, hardware ordering, dev access, training, testing.

questions.question_text: Walk me through a typical day at the center, open to close. What time do doors open and close? What happens when someone walks in? Who greets them? Is there a front desk?
questions.helper_text: Start here. Understand the flow before designing anything. Note the rhythm of the day — busy times, slow times, transitions.

questions.question_text: How do clients currently sign in? Paper log, Excel, nothing? What information is captured at sign-in today? Who handles it — the client or staff?
questions.helper_text: This is the process we're replacing. Understand it thoroughly before improving it.

questions.question_text: How are new clients registered today? What information do you collect on first visit? Is there an intake form? How long does registration take?
questions.helper_text: Get a copy of the intake form if one exists. Note the distinction between first-visit and returning-client workflows.

questions.question_text: What services does the center offer? Which services need to be tracked per visit? Are any services limited (e.g., one shower per day)?
questions.helper_text: List all services: meals, showers, laundry, case management, mail, phone, computer access, etc. Services with limits drive scheduling logic.

questions.question_text: How many clients per day, roughly? Average day? Busiest day? How many total unique clients over a year?
questions.helper_text: Sizing data. Affects database design, scroll list length, and performance requirements.

questions.question_text: Who are the staff members who will use this system? How many staff? What roles? Volunteers? Who is the day-to-day system owner?
questions.helper_text: Identify the primary contact and system champion. Also note if volunteers need limited access.

questions.question_text: What reports do you run today? Daily counts? Monthly summaries? Who reads these reports (board, funders, staff)? What format (printed, emailed, uploaded)?
questions.helper_text: Reports are the output of the system. If we don't know what comes out, we can't design what goes in.

questions.question_text: What are your funding sources? HUD homeless assistance grants (triggers HMIS)? Ryan White HIV/AIDS Program (triggers RSR)? SAMHSA? State or county contracts? Private foundations?
questions.helper_text: Funding sources dictate reporting requirements. This is the most consequential question for system design.

questions.question_text: Do you currently report to HMIS? Which regional CoC? Which HMIS vendor (Clarity, ServicePoint, other)? Who does the data entry? How and how often?
questions.helper_text: If they report to HMIS, we need to understand the current workflow so we can export compatible data.

questions.question_text: Do you report Ryan White / RSR data? Do you use CAREWare? Who handles RSR reporting?
questions.helper_text: Ryan White reporting has specific data requirements that affect client data fields.

questions.question_text: What other reporting obligations exist? Funder-specific reports? Internal board reports? County or state reporting?
questions.helper_text: Catch-all for any reporting requirements not covered by HMIS or RSR.

questions.question_text: Is there existing data to migrate? Excel spreadsheets? Access database? How far back? How clean is it? Can I get a copy (de-identified if needed)?
questions.helper_text: Data migration scope affects timeline and cost. Dirty data = more work.

questions.question_text: What demographic fields are needed at registration? For each: is it required by a funder? (First/last name, preferred name, DOB, SSN, gender identity, race, ethnicity, veteran status, disabling condition, prior living situation)
questions.helper_text: Start with the minimum. For each field ask: Do you need this? Is it required by a funder? Fewer fields = faster registration = fewer errors.

questions.question_text: What contact and program-specific fields are needed? (Phone, email, emergency contact, insurance status, income level, household composition, referral source)
questions.helper_text: Contact info may not be available for all clients. Consider which fields are truly necessary vs nice-to-have.

questions.question_text: HIV status collection: Does your grant reporting require tracking HIV status at the individual client level? Or does the center serve people who may be living with HIV without recording that specifically?
questions.helper_text: Most sensitive data question. Options: (a) Don't collect it — safest, nothing to protect. (b) Collect as yes/no for grant compliance. (c) Collect with detail. Recommendation: option (a) unless a funder explicitly requires it. Under ORS 433.045, HIV data has heightened confidentiality protections.

questions.question_text: What operational fields are needed? Photo for ID/recognition? Client ID card issued? Notes/comments field? Case manager assignment?
questions.helper_text: Photos add complexity (storage, privacy). ID cards add a physical process. Notes fields are useful but need access control.

questions.question_text: What check-in fields should be captured at each visit? (Services used, check-out time, notes, reason for visit, referrals made)
questions.helper_text: Date/time is automatic. Services come from the service list. Consider: does check-out happen (manual or auto at close)?

questions.question_text: Describe the physical layout of the center. Is there a front desk or reception area? Where would clients check in? Where do staff sit? Is the check-in area in line of sight of staff?
questions.helper_text: This determines deployment topology. Draw a sketch if helpful. Note distances between client area and staff area.

questions.question_text: Is there a desk where both a client screen and a staff screen could sit side by side (Topology 1)? Or does staff admin need to be in a separate room from client check-in (Topology 2)?
questions.helper_text: Topology 1 (side-by-side) is simpler — one PC, two screens. Topology 2 (separate rooms) requires two devices + network.

questions.question_text: Power situation: Are there outlets at the check-in location? Is the power reliable? Surges or outages? Does the center have a UPS or surge protection?
questions.helper_text: A UPS is cheap insurance. If power is unreliable, factor this into hardware choice (tablet with battery = free UPS).

questions.question_text: Internet: Is there WiFi at the center? The kiosk does NOT need internet for daily operation. But is occasional internet available for software updates? Or is the system truly air-gapped?
questions.helper_text: Kiosk operates offline. Internet is only needed for updates and optional data export. Note WiFi coverage quality.

questions.question_text: Security of the device: Is the check-in area supervised at all times? Risk of theft or tampering? Does the device need to be locked down or bolted?
questions.helper_text: Wall-mounted screen is inherently more secure than a device on a table. Consider cable locks, VESA mounts, tamper-resistant enclosures.

questions.question_text: Single screen or dual screen? Single: staff and clients share one screen with PIN mode switch. Dual: client screen + staff screen (recommended if there's a front desk).
questions.helper_text: Given that staff sit in a separate room, this affects whether the kiosk is standalone or networked to the staff office.

questions.question_text: Hardware tier preference? Tier 1: All-in-One touchscreen PC (~$400-700). Tier 2: Windows tablet + locking stand (~$300-600). Tier 3: Mini PC + touchscreen monitor (~$350-600).
questions.helper_text: Miss Cobine wants a wall-mounted screen. This favors Tier 3 (mini PC hidden + wall-mounted touch monitor) or a wall-mountable AIO.

questions.question_text: New or refurbished hardware? Refurbished business AIO: ~$250-350. New: ~$400-700.
questions.helper_text: Refurb saves money but shorter warranty. For a mission-critical kiosk, new with 3-year warranty may be worth it.

questions.question_text: Do you want spare hardware on hand? A second mini PC or spare AIO means same-day recovery. Without it, recovery depends on sourcing a replacement.
questions.helper_text: USB backup drive + install media can restore the system onto ANY Windows 11 Pro PC. Spare hardware is insurance.

questions.question_text: Software path: C# native Windows app or Python/Flask via WSL2?
questions.helper_text: Internal decision — may not need client input. C# recommended for fewer moving parts and more reliable startup. Python if portability to Pi matters or developer is significantly more productive in Python.

questions.question_text: How many staff accounts are needed? What permission levels make sense? (Proposed: Admin, Staff, Kiosk)
questions.helper_text: Proposed roles: Admin (full access, user management, settings), Staff (register clients, view records, run reports), Kiosk (client-facing check-in only, no login required).

questions.question_text: How should the client check-in screen work? (a) Client enters a code from an ID card, (b) Client types their name and selects from matches, (c) Client enters DOB + last name, (d) Staff checks them in (not self-service).
questions.helper_text: Consider the population: some clients cannot read or write. The current approach is a scrolling list of first name + last initial. Support both keyboard filtering AND scrolling the full list.

questions.question_text: Can clients self-register, or must staff do it? What should happen if someone isn't in the system?
questions.helper_text: Current process: new clients get a free first day (guest pass) and are directed to staff office. Registration entered on second visit. This is a deliberate workflow, not a gap.

questions.question_text: Data retention: 10-year unified policy? (Satisfies Oregon medical, HIPAA, HMIS, federal grants.) Confirm or adjust.
questions.helper_text: 10 years covers the most conservative requirement. Shorter is simpler but may miss a grant obligation.

questions.question_text: Backup rotation: weekly swap (Drive A/B) + annual archive (Drive C)? Confirm or adjust.
questions.helper_text: Simple, physical, air-gapped. Staff swaps USB drives weekly. Annual drive goes in a safe.

questions.question_text: Open source or proprietary code? Open source (MIT): reusable, no lock-in, community. Proprietary: EMO owns exclusively.
questions.helper_text: Recommendation is open source. Benefits other shelters. No competitive disadvantage for EMO.

questions.question_text: Who holds the database decryption password? Which staff member(s)? Where is it stored (sealed envelope in a safe)? Who is the backup if that person leaves?
questions.helper_text: Key custody is an organizational decision. Needs at least two people who know the password. Written backup in a sealed envelope in a safe is standard.

questions.question_text: Data incident procedure: Does EMO have an existing data breach policy? If a USB drive is lost, who gets notified?
questions.helper_text: Encrypted USB drives mitigate breach impact. But the policy should exist before it's needed.

questions.question_text: When do you want the system operational? Is there a hard deadline (grant cycle, fiscal year)? Or 'as soon as practical'?
questions.helper_text: Hard deadlines constrain everything. 'As soon as practical' gives room to do it right.

questions.question_text: Hardware ordering: Does EMO purchase directly, or do I order and invoice? Who approves the purchase? Ship to the center or to me for setup?
questions.helper_text: Shipping to developer for setup means the system arrives ready to install. Shipping to center means one fewer handoff.

questions.question_text: Development access: Who is my primary point of contact at EMO? Best way to reach them for questions during build?
questions.helper_text: Need a responsive contact for questions that come up during development. Email, phone, text?

questions.question_text: Training: How many staff need training? On-site training at deployment? Written guide sufficient for ongoing reference?
questions.helper_text: Plan for on-site training at go-live plus a written guide. Consider staff turnover — new hires need to learn too.

questions.question_text: Testing: Can I deploy a test version at the center before going live? Is there a quiet period to do a soft launch?
questions.helper_text: A week of parallel running (kiosk + paper clipboard) builds confidence before cutting over.

questions.question_text: Wall-mounted display preference: Miss Cobine wants the screen mounted on the wall to free up table space. What size? What height? Does the wall location have power and line-of-sight to staff?
questions.helper_text: Wall mount changes the hardware calculus. Need a VESA-compatible touchscreen monitor + hidden mini PC, or a wall-mountable AIO. Also consider: can clients comfortably reach and use a wall-mounted touchscreen?

questions.question_text: Information radiator: What events, calendar items, schedules, and brochure content should display on the kiosk screen when not in use for check-in?
questions.helper_text: Miss Cobine wants the screen to replace table brochures with digital content — events, calendar, service schedules. This is a screensaver/idle mode feature. Staff need a way to update this content.

questions.question_text: Shower and laundry scheduling: Should clients be able to pick an available time slot for showers or laundry at check-in? How does this notify staff? How many slots per time period?
questions.helper_text: Nice-to-have feature from Miss Cobine. Requires: time slot configuration, availability display, booking, staff notification. Could be Phase 2 if it adds too much to Phase 1 scope.
